title: A Bioelectronic Sensor for Rapid Assessment of Antibody Titer and Glycan Structure
description: This project will result in a scalable, microfluidic system for rapid and informative analytical measurements that will bolster the development of vaccines and therapeutics for coronavirus.
text:
The biomanufacturing industry would benefit from rapid, scalable, and informative analytical measurements for use during biologics production that would speed up the development and production of vaccines and therapeutics.
The University of Maryland, College Park, aimed to develop a microfluidic analytic device for the rapid quantification of antibody titer and N-linked galactosylation. The device is integrated with bioelectronic sensor technology that can quickly inform the total proportion of galactosylated antibodies in a sample. If successful, this device would reduce cost and time associated with the development and production processes of biologics by generating rapid analysis of vital analytical features of antibody therapeutics.
The implementation of this bioelectronic microfluidic device transforms biologics analytics from a multi-day laboratory process into a rapid, real-time measurement, potentially reducing analytical lead times by over 85%. By utilizing regenerable sensing surfaces and low-cost 3D-printed architecture, the platform can lower the cost-per-assay by an estimated 60–75% compared to traditional HPLC-based glycan analysis. Ultimately, these efficiencies protect high-value batches from quality drift and accelerate the overall CMC timeline, significantly increasing the Net Present Value of the therapeutic asset.
